text.fullText: From: Jim Steitz <noreply@myfor.ms> To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/12/2026 9:46:39 AM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 ------------------------------------------ Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. The Clean Water Act makes plain that states may veto Section 401 permits for infrastructure projects that cause gratuitous harm to aquatic ecosystems in that state, correctly reflected in the 1971 rule. The purpose of this clause is vividly demonstrated today. The authors of the Clean Water Act anticipated that one day, the EPA leadership offices would be occupied by scientifically and morally illiterate men, ambitious for power and favor, who say EPA’s mission is to unleash American industry. They would purge EPA of its responsibility to protect our streams and wetlands from economic interests, and instead join industry in shuffling along this destruction, disdainful and oblivious to the destruction of biodiversity, hydrological systems, and human health. This has now come to pass. They anticipated that one day, vapid, hand-waving appeals to nebulous concepts like ‘vital infrastructure,’ or ‘national interest,’ or this Administration’s fetish, ‘energy dominance,’ could be wielded to discard Americans’ love for the very things that make life worth living, and make America a worthwhile place to live. In my former home state of Oregon, the Department of Environmental Quality vetoed the so-called ‘Jordan Cove Liquified Natural Gas’ Project in 2019 using its section 401 authority. This was a correct choice, and the people of Oregon and America are better off for it. EPA would define out of existence the capacity of Oregon to choose whether it wishes to be a sacrifice zone. This cautionary scenario is repeatable any time that a company with no care either for the people and life of a place, or for the future, draw a line on a map showing all to be bulldozed, erasing everything beautiful, precious, and valuable in the way. Section 401 is a tool for humans with values not measured in dollars or in barrels or cubic feet to assert their right to a quality of life, and to a healthy local ecosystem. America has lost the majority of its wetlands already. The extinction crisis is especially pronounced in aquatic ecosystems. Simultaneously, extremes of flood and drought are magnified by climate change, whether this Administration believes it or not. Our remaining wetlands, aquifers, and tributary streams are more precious than ever in buffering these extremes. This Section 401 guardrail against combined, federal-corporate voracious power is more vital than ever. If EPA hands industry the tools to steamroll the love of Americans for our last aquatic ecosystems, the Trump Administration will leave our children with great ‘energy dominance,’ but a bleak, barren, meaningless world stripped of natural life, and a traumatic, harried struggle for survival between droughts and floods. Sincerely, Jim Steitz St. Louis, MO
